coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activities of commercial P25 TiO2 by trapping holes and transferring electrons for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Wu, Jing
Lu, Hongwei
Wang, Jinshuang
Qu, Yang
Jing, Liqiang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, commercial P25 TiO2 was co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions by a simple impregnation method, and then coupled with carbon quantum dots with a wet-chemical process. It is demonstrated that the photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2 for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation are improved obviously after co-modifying chloride and phosphate anions and then coupling with carbon dots. It is confirmed that the modified chloride and phosphate anions could effectively trap photogenerated holes, while the coupled carbon dots could facilitate photogenerated electrons transfer based on the steady-state surface photovoltage spectra, I–V curves, the electrochemical impedance spectra and the formed ·OH amounts, implying that the photogenerated charge separation of P25 TiO2 is greatly enhanced, leading to the obviously-improved activities. This work would help us develop a much feasible route to improve the photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P25 TiO2
Trapping hole
Transferring electron
Charge separation
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (A) and DRS spectra (B) of different samples.
TEM and HRTEM images of 0.1P/0.3Cl/TP25 (A) and 2C/0.1P/0.3Cl/TP25 (B).
Photocatalytic activities on different samples for CO2 conversion without co-catalyst in pH=7 water (A) and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation (B).

SPS responses in N2 (A), I–V curves (B) in dark (black lines) and under light irradiation (colored lines), Nyquist plots for electrochemical impedance spectra (C) and fluorescence spectra related to hydroxyl radicals after irradiation for 1h by the widely-used coumarin fluorescent method (D) on different samples.
Scheme for the photogenerated charge transfer and separation of P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions, and then coupled with CQDs, along with the induced photochemical reactions.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activities of commercial P25 TiO2 by trapping holes and transferring electrons for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Materials Chemistry (Heilongjiang University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Harbin 150080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Materials Chemistry (Heilongjiang University)
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 Charge separation is enhanced by transferring electrons and trapping holes for improved photoactivities to convert CO2 and degrade pollutant.

In this work, commercial P25 TiO2 was co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions by a simple impregnation method, and then coupled with carbon quantum dots with a wet-chemical process. It is demonstrated that the photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2 for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation are improved obviously after co-modifying chloride and phosphate anions and then coupling with carbon dots. It is confirmed that the modified chloride and phosphate anions could effectively trap photogenerated holes, while the coupled carbon dots could facilitate photogenerated electrons transfer based on the steady-state surface photovoltage spectra, I–V curves, the electrochemical impedance spectra and the formed ·OH amounts, implying that the photogenerated charge separation of P25 TiO2 is greatly enhanced, leading to the obviously-improved activities. This work would help us develop a much feasible route to improve the photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: In the 21st century, vast of energy crisis and environmental issues that result from the fast development of global social economy turn up. The chlorophenols arising from the waste water of factories are highly hard to degrade and dramatically harmful to the human’s health and the environment, especially for the 2,4-dichlorophenol [1,2]. In addition, the greenhouse effect caused by the increased CO2 concentration in atmosphere from the combustion of fossil fuels, vehicle exhaust and factories has brought a series of environment issues. Thus, the conversion of CO2 into sustainable chemical fuels is highly desirable. Among numerous technologies for degradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol and conversion of CO2, semiconductor photocatalysts stand out owing to its advantages of cheapness, stability and free secondary pollution [3,4]. However, it also has some shortages, the photocatalytic activities of semiconductors are limited owing to its low photogenerated charge separation [5,6]. Noble metal cocatalyst loading, semiconductor coupling and inorganic acids modification are traditional feasible methods to solve this bottleneck problem.

By loading noble metal, the photogenerated electrons that result from semiconductor could be captured and further promote the charge separation [7,8]. By coupling semiconductors, the photogenerated electrons that result from one semiconductor could transfer to another semiconductor to further promote the charge separation [9–12]. By inorganic acid modification, the formed negative field on the surfaces would favor the photogenerated holes trapped, thus leading to the enhanced charge separation and then to the improved activity [13,14]. However, all these methods aim to improve the photocatalytic activities by one-way regulation either from the photogenerated electrons or holes. Few published works have been involved with the two-way regulation simultaneously up to date. In fact, it is not fully clear for the nature of the photocatalytic process, in which two half-reactions initiated by photogenerated electrons and holes are equally important. In our previous work, we had synthesized boron-doped g-C3N4 nanosheets and then coupled with anatase TiO2, it is demonstrated that the enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activities of g-C3N4 is attributed to the surface states formed from the doped B to trap holes, and to the coupled TiO2 to accept the photogenerated electrons so as to improve the charge separation of g-C3N4 [15]. Hence, it is vital to adjust and control the photogenerated electrons and holes respectively to improve the photogenerated charge separation by bi-directional regulation in the photocatalytic process.

Among numerous photocatalysts investigated, TiO2 is one of the most widespread photocatalysts [16,17]. Especially, the mixed crystal P25 TiO2, in which the mass ratio of anatase and rutile is approximate 80/20, shows excellent photocatalytic activity, on account of the two mixed structures increase the lattice defect density of TiO2 crystal and the concentration of the carrier, leading to high photogenerated charge separation [18–20]. However, its photocatalytic activity is still not high enough for practical applications. Hence, it is important to further promote its photogenerated charge separation. Ren et al. has synthesized P25-graphene nanocomposite with high photocatalytic activities, which is attributed to the regulation of photogenerated electrons since the coupled graphene could accept the electrons from P25 to enhance the charge separation [21]. Yasuhiro et al. have prepared Pt/P25 photocatalyst, which showed efficient aerobic oxidation of aniline to nitrosobenzene [22]. Not hard to see, it is always emphasized on improving the photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2 by one-way regulation, and few works have payed attention to bi-directional regulation of photogenerated electrons and holes for efficient photocatalysis.

In general, it is feasible to introduce surface polarization on semiconductors to trap photogenerated holes so as to promote the charge separation since holes are positively carried. In our previous work, we have successfully improved the activities for photoelectrochemical water oxidation of BiVO4 by the formed surface negative field after phosphate modification [13]. We have also synthesized rutile TiO2 nanorods with the modified chloride on the surfaces, and it is demonstrated that the modified chloride could effectively trap photogenerated holes so as to promote the charge separation, leading to the exceptional photoactivities for PEC water oxidation to evolve O2 [14]. Therefore, it is feasible for trapping holes to introduce the surface polarization to P25 TiO2 for further enhanced photocatalytic activities. However, no related reports have been found to date. In order to facilitate the photogenerated electrons transfer, nano-carbon materials are often used to couple to semiconductors as acceptors. In recent years, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been employed in combining with many semiconductors for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in water [23–26]. Di. et al. has synthesized CQDs modified BiOCl ultrathin nanosheet photocatalyst, and the improved photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the superior electron transfer ability [27]. Recently, we have successfully synthesized rutile TiO2 nanorods co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions, which exhibited efficient cocatalyst-free photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to fuels. Interestingly, the photocatalytic performance is further improved after coupling with CNTs as electron acceptors [28]. Hence, it is a possible way to collect electrons by coupled CQDs from P25 TiO2 for improved photoactivities.

Unfortunately, to the best of our knowledge, few works related to introduced surface polarization groups on P25 TiO2 by treating with inorganic acids and coupling with CQDs to improve the charge separation from the view point of bi-directional regulation have been reported to date. Herein, we try to co-modify commercial P25 TiO2 with chloride and phosphate anions, and then further couple with CQDs. It is demonstrated that the bi-directional regulation could enhance the charge separation, leading to the greatly-improved photocatalytic activities of P25 TiO2 for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation. This work would provide us with a much feasible route to improve the photocatalytic activities of TiO2 for energy production and environmental remediation.
All the chemicals in this study were of analytical grade and used as-received without further purification. Deionized water was used through-out the experiments.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Synthesis of P25 TiO2 modified with chloride ions: 0.5g of P25 TiO2 powder was dispersed into 35ml of different concentration of NaCl solution. Then, the solution was vigorously stirred for 1h to obtain white suspension. Subsequently, the suspension was placed in Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactors and heated at 160°C for 6h. After that, a white precipitate was collected and washed repeatedly with isopropanol and distilled water. Finally, the Cl-modified P25 TiO2 sample referred to as XCl/TP25 was obtained by drying and calcining at 450°C for 0.5h, in which X is the concentration of NaCl solution used.

Synthesis of P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride ions and phosphate anions: to co-modify P25 TiO2 with chloride ions and phosphate anions, 0.5g of 0.3Cl/TP25 (0.3 means the concentration of NaCl solution used) powder was dispersed into 20ml of different concentration of H3PO4 solution. Then, the solution was vigorously stirred for 2h, centrifuged and washed with deionized water. Finally, the reaction mixture was dried in a convection oven and then calcined at 450°C for 0.5h. Thus, the sample of P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride ions and phosphate anions referred to as XP/0.3Cl/TP25 was obtained, in which X represents the concentration of phosphate used.

Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs): 0.6g of graphite was dispersed into 30ml of concentrated nitric acid, then added 90ml of concentrated sulfuric acid slowly, condensed at 120°C for 8h, cooled down to the room temperature, and then adjusted the pH to 7 by adding NaOH solution, dialysis, filtration, centrifugation, dry.

Synthesis of P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride ions and phosphate anions, and coupled with carbon quantum dots (CQDs): 0.5g of XP/0.3Cl/TP25 powder was dispersed into 20ml of distilled water, then 2% (2% means the mass ratio percentage of CQDs to P25 TiO2) of CQDs were taken and dissolved in the mixed solution above. The mixture was centrifuged for 10min and then stirred for 4h, dried at 80°C. Thus, P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride ions and phosphate anions, and coupled with carbon quantum dots were obtained referred to 2C/XP/0.3Cl/TP25. C in 2C means the coupled CQDs, and 2 is the mass ratio percentage of CQDs to P25 TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: The samples were analyzed by using various techniques. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were measured with a Rigaku D/MAX-rA powder diffractometer, using Cu Kα radiation. TEM images of various samples were taken by a JEM-3010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 300kV. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were recorded with a Model Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer, using BaSO4 as reference. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was used to detect the surface composition and elemental chemical state of the samples, by using a Model VG ESCALAB apparatus with Mg K X-ray source, and the binding energies were calibrated with respect to the signal for adventitious carbon (binding energy=284.6eV). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the samples were collected with a Bruker Equinox 55 Spectrometer, using KBr as diluents. The surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS) measurements of the various samples were carried out with a home-built apparatus [16]. PEC experiments were performed in a glass cell with 0.5M NaClO4 solution as the electrolyte. In a typical glass cell, the as-prepared film electrode functioned as working electrode, Pt wire (99.9%) functioned as a counter electrode, and a saturated KCl Ag/AgCl electrode functioned as the reference electrode. High purity nitrogen gas (99.999%) was used to bubble through the electrolyte before and during the experiments. Applied potentials were controlled by a commercial computer-controlled potentiostat (LK2006A made in China). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed using a three-electrode configuration with the Princeton Applied Research Versa STAT 3 and carried out over the frequency range from 0.5 to 1×105 Hz with an amplitude of 10mV (RMS) in a 0.5M NaClO4 solution, using a 300W xenon light as the illumination source. Besides, we also carried out the measurements of formed hydroxyl radical. To detect the amount of hydroxyl radical, 0.02g of the powder sample was dispersed into 50ml of 1×10−3 mol/L aqueous solution in a quartz reactor. At given irradiation time, a certain amount of the centrifuged transparent solution was transferred into a Pyrex glass cell for the fluorescence measurement of 7-hydroxycoumarin at around 456nm under the excitation wavelength of 332nm with a spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer LS 55).

0.2g of powder samples were dispersed in pH=7 water (3ml) contained in a cylindrical steel reactor with 100ml volume and 3.5cm2 areas. A 300W Xenon arc lamp was used as the light source. High pure CO2 gas was passed through water and then entered into the reaction setup for reaching ambient pressure. The used photocatalyst was allowed to equilibrate in the CO2/H2O system for 1h. During irradiation, about 0.5ml of gas production was taken from the reaction cell at given time interval for subsequent CH4 concentration analysis by using a gas chromatograph (GC-2014 with FID and TCD detector, Shimadzu Corp, Japan).

0.05g of the samples were dispersed in 30ml of 10mg/l 2,4-dichlorophenol solution, and then irradiated for 1h. After centrifugation, the 2,4-dichlorophenol concentration of each sample was measured according to the characteristic optical absorption of 285nm with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of different samples are shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. S1. One can see that the XRD peaks at the values of 25.3° and 27.4° can be indexed to the (101) face of anatase and (110) face of rutile TiO2 [29], and the modification with chloride anions and phosphate anions and CQDs does not change the crystal phase of P25 TiO2. And also, the chloride anions and phosphate anions modification does not change the optical absorption of TiO2. However, the optical absorption of visible-light region of CQDs-coupled P25 TiO2 have been found(Fig. 1B and Fig. S2). From the FTIR spectra in Fig. S3, we can see that the peak results from stretching vibrations of [PO4]3− groups appears at about 1020cm−1 [28]. From the enlarged inset of the region from 1000 to 2000cm−1 of raman spectra(Fig. S4), two characteristic Raman active modes can be seen, it is attributed to G-band at about 1587cm −1 and D-band at around 1357cm −1 of CQDs, respectively [30].

According to the XPS measurements, the chemical states of all the elements in different samples are investigated as shown in Fig. S5. It can be seen clearly, that all the samples contain Ti and O. And the samples that modified with chloride anions also contain Cl element. About the Ti element, there are two forms stemming from Ti4+ which are observed at 464.1eV and 458.5eV for Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively. In the O1s XPS spectra, we can see a strong peak at 529.5eV which is attributed to the Ti-O-Ti lattice oxygen in TiO2. In the P2p XPS spectra, strong peaks which are centered at 133.6eV for the phosphate-treated TiO2 can be seen, which are attributed to the characteristic of the pentavalent oxidation state (P5+) as the form of the P-O bond [31,32]. Notably, the binding energies of Ti2p and O1s of untreated TiO2 are a little lower compared with treated one, especially for Ti 2p. This is attributed to the replace of −Ti-OH with −Ti-O-P-OH after modification with phosphate group, indicating that the surface chemistry of TiO2 has been changed.

In order to further investigate the crystal size and morphology of modified samples, TEM images were taken as shown in Fig. 2 . One can find from Fig. 2A that the facet distances of 0.325nm and 0.35nm corresponding to rutile TiO2 (110) anatase and TiO2 (101), respectively [33,34]. In Fig. 2B, several dark dots with diameters of about 4nm can be seen which is attributed to the CQDs. Obviously, it is confirmed that the P25 TiO2 have been modified with chloride and phosphate anions and CQDs successfully.

Based on the above conclusions, we have succeed in modifying chloride and phosphate anions and coupling CQDs on P25 TiO2, so it is supposed that the photoactivities of TiO2 would enhance greatly. In order to confirm the suspect, the photocatalytic activities for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation were tested, as shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S6. It is clearly to see that the photoactivity of P25 TiO2 is improved after modified with chloride, and the photoactivity enhanced along with the increased chloride amount. 0.3Cl/TP25 shows the best performance among the samples. However, the performance begins to become weak if the used chloride amount is excess. Similarly, the moderate introduction of phosphate groups also support the improved photoactivity, and 0.1P/0.3Cl/TP25 sample shows the highest activity. Undoubtedly, after coupling with appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots, the photocatalytic activities continue to be enhanced, with ∼4-time enhancement compared to that of P25 TiO2.

With xenon illumination, the photogenerated electrons of TiO2 are excited from the valence band to the conduction band, yielding positive holes in the valence band and negative electrons in the conduction band. The photogenerated charge separation would greatly determine the final photocatalytic activities. The surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS) is an effective way to investigate the charge separation of semiconduct photocatalyst deeply, especially for the atmosphere-controlled SPS one. Since CO2 conversion was carried out without the existence of O2, it is meaningful to make use of the N2-controlled SPS responses to record the photogenerated charge separation of treated and unterated samples. From Fig. 4 A and Fig. S7, one can see that no SPS responses can be found for pure P25 TiO2 because of no donators to trap holes. An appropriate amount of chloride improve the SPS response of P25 TiO2 greatly, which is attributed to the modified chloride on the surface of P25 TiO2 as donators to trap holes. And the introduction of phosphate has little impact on the SPS intensity. For the chloride and phosphate co-modified P25 TiO2, its SPS intensity nearly keep unchanged compared with chloride-modified P25 TiO2. This is because that the modified phosphate groups do not wok in N2 atmosphere without charges for charge separation. It is worthy of noting that the modified phosphate groups on TiO2 could dissociate in water to form the surface negative field so as to trap positive holes [16]. Moreover, the SPS response of 0.1P/0.3Cl/TP25 could be enhanced after coupling with an appropriate amount of CQDs. The enhanced SPS response is attributed to the coupled CQDs as acceptors to transfer electrons and transport charges.

In general, a higher photogenerated charge separation will correspond to a higher photocurrent densities. In order to further study the mechanism, the I–V curves in dark and under light irradiation were also carried out. As shown in Fig. 4B, the modification of chloride anions enhanced the photocurrent density of P25 TiO2 and the introduction of phosphate and CQDs make the photocurrent density continue to increase, the regularities keep consistent with the photogenerated charge separation of samples. However, there was no current response in dark for all the samples. From the electrochemical impedance spectra, we can see that the radius of TP25, 0.3Cl/TP25, 0.2P/0.3Cl/TP25, 2C/0.2P/0.3Cl/TP25 gradually decreased. In general, the smaller radius, the smaller charge transfer resistance. During the photocatalytic processes for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation, the produced hydroxyl radical (OH) as the key intermediates of the hole-water reaction could reflect the charge separation of photocatalyst. Therefore, the amount of formed OH was employed to measure by a widely-used coumarin fluorescent method, as shown in Fig. 4C and Fig. S8. It is demonstrated that the formed OH amounts agree well with the photocurrent density results.

Based on the above discussion, to clearly understand the mechanism for the photogenerated charge transfer and separation of P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions, and then coupled with CQDs, a feasible scheme is suggested, as shown in Fig. 5 . When the sample was illuminated with xenon lamp, there are photogenerated electrons and holes yielding in the conduction band and valence band of TiO2. The photogenerated electrons could react with OH− and/or H2O in the solution to form OH radicals. The OH radicals with strong oxidizability would degrade 2,4-dichlorophenol into CO2. The photogenerated electrons would react with CO2 to produce clean energy. The negative field formed by phosphate anions in water and the modified chloride on the surfaces of P25 TiO2 would trap holes together, and the coupled CQDs could effectively transfer electrons, so as to promote the charge separation. Therefore, it is concluded that the greatly-enhanced charge separation is well responsible for the obviously-improved photocatalytic activities for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized P25 TiO2 co-modified with chloride and phosphate anions, and then coupled with CQDs. The modified chloride and phosphate anions could trap holes, while the coupled CQDs could transfer photogenerated electrons. As a result, the photogenerated charge separation could be greatly enhanced. This leads to obviously-improved photocatalytic activities for CO2 conversion and 2,4-dichlorophenol degradation. This work would help us deep understand the photogenerated charge properties and provide with a feasible strategy to synthesize efficient TiO2-based photocatalysts for CO2 conversion and for pollutant degradation.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the key NSFC (U1401245, 91622119), the National Key Basic Research Program of China (2014CB660814), the Project of Chinese Ministry of Education (213011A), and the Science Foundation for Excellent Youth of Harbin City of China (2014RFYXJ002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.04.004.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
